cases.title: Complaint of Mueller v. South Florida Presbytery
cases.synopsis: The complaint was withdrawn by agreement of the complainant, the Presbytery Clerk, and the Chairman of Presbytery's Judicial Commission on December 21, 2023. The SJC approved the request and remanded the matter to Presbytery without instructions and without prejudice.

cases.title: Moehn v. Westminster Presbytery
cases.synopsis: TE Andy Moehn complained that Westminster Presbytery erred in its BCO 31-2 investigation of his wife Kacey Moehn regarding alleged desertion and in declaring the question of biblical divorce grounds moot. The SJC sustained the complaint 21-1-0, finding that Presbytery used an extra-confessional interpretation of WCF 24.6 requiring excommunication before desertion grounds could apply, dismissed relevant evidence, and improperly adjudicated TE Moehn's role in the marriage breakdown without due process; the SJC annulled Presbytery's Recommendations 1 and 3 and found a new investigation was not in order since Mrs. Moehn had left PCA jurisdiction.

cases.title: Gleason v. Houston Metro Presbytery
cases.synopsis: Mr. Gleason filed a BCO 43 complaint alleging the Session sinned by approving a Personnel Committee Statement read to the congregation regarding an associate pastor's call dissolution. The SJC ruled the complaint judicially out of order and unable to be put in order because BCO 43 complaints are not a constitutionally permissible avenue for processing accusations of individual sin or requiring repentance from a church court, as complaints address errors of courts rather than sins of individuals.

cases.title: Gozé v. Hills and Plains Presbytery
cases.synopsis: The SJC reviewed the complaint and found it administratively out of order and unable to be put in order because it was not a complaint against an act of a court (the Session).

cases.title: Bigelow v. North Florida Presbytery
cases.synopsis: TE Kevin Bigelow complained against multiple actions of North Florida Presbytery related to the dissolution of his call as Assistant Pastor of Christ Church East (where he was also serving as planting pastor at Christ Church Beaches). The SJC sustained the complaint in part (22-0-0), finding that Presbytery erred in ruling four of Bigelow's five complaints out of order; however, after reviewing the substance of all five complaints, the SJC denied each on the merits, concluding Presbytery committed no clear error in dissolving the call, that no congregational vote of CCB was constitutionally required, and that no formal discipline without process had occurred.
